evidence_quotes.quote: People who are not already in their broken EULA system are simply mislead into thinking they bought it.
During this time, i've seen plenty of games being not only delisted from the stores when publisher wanted to, but also having access removed, making it impossible to run the game at all.
If you have started the download (not even fully downloaded it or played it) you are no longer eligible for a refund.
EULA is presented only AFTER you have made a purchase for physical games and tried to play it for the first time.
However, every single license I have signed (not related to videogames) had a start and end date written on it.
they can cancel the licence anytime without any reason (as per their own EULA example: Activision Blizzard) which is extremaly one sided, and shouldn't be allowed.

paragraphs.text: To whom it might concern. I have played Videogames my entire life, started when I was five years old. Now i'm 32, and i'm still in love with videogames. It has become my hobby, just like any other, and as any hobby can become expensive. Sadly I can't run a lot of them as they were taken away by publishers. They can take away anything at any time, no matter how much customers paid. During this time, i've seen plenty of games being not only delisted from the stores when publisher wanted to, but also having access removed, making it impossible to run the game at all. Imagine buying a pool table, playing it for a while and next day it's gone, because the manufacturer has decided they no longer make this model. Think it's like that with golf clubs, you bought a whole set, played a few games with it and on the next day, half of your clubs are gone or even the whole set. For me, it's not okay. It wasn't a rental, someone just broke into your personal space and took it. This can happen because there's no (or very little) regulation for Videogames and their preservation. Publishers are trying to walk around the current law saying that they are selling a licence, not a product. However, every single license I have signed (not related to videogames) had a start and end date written on it. With Videogames there's no date or anything that would indicate an end of live/support on the day of payment, some companies can cancel the licence anytime without any reason (as per their own EULA example: Activision Blizzard) which is extremaly one sided, and shouldn't be allowed. Since it's beginning, Videogames industry was self-regulating, this lead to misuse and as a result companies like Sony allow two-week refunds only if you have not started downloading the product. If you have started the download (not even fully downloaded it or played it) you are no longer eligible for a refund. Absolutely disregarding currently existing Right to withdrawal for Digital products. Most of the times when you are buying physical games you will not be presented with EULA before unboxing the game, inserting a disc (or activating a key). EULA is presented only AFTER you have made a purchase for physical games and tried to play it for the first time. The physical stores do not inform you about it either, they take your money and give you the game. By default, most of the consumers (or people not interested in videogames who want to give someone game as a gift) would think they are buying it as any other physical product. People who are not already in their broken EULA system are simply mislead into thinking they bought it. There are many examples of this kind of behaviour where current laws are walked around, we need regulations in videogames just like with any other aspect of our life, self-regulation is no longer working as their greed increases. Video games industry is the biggest entertainment industry in the world, beating Movies and Music, increasing every single year and yet it's still being ignored by the lawmakers because "it's for kids". In my eyes, Videogames are just like any other form of art - you'll get a great audio-visual experience, stories that do stand out from books or movies, experience that you can navigate and have an impact on, that can change because of your interaction with the world. It's hundreds if not thousands of work from multiple people, from different nations, origins, believes that can be taken away from you even if you paid for it, whenever publishers please. Please add Videogames to your Digital Fairness Act, as whatever we have today is not Fairness. Kind regards, Piotr.
